serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of fresh mint leaves is approximately 5 g (about 1 tablespoon), used as a garnish or flavouring in beverages, salads, and cooked dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Mint, fresh — Nutritional composition per 100 g
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central (cross-validated)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95). Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173474 (Spearmint, fresh). Minor differences in potassium and calcium are within expected natural variation; HPB values retained as primary. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FDC 173474 (0.19 g/100 g, rounded to 0.2 g) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of herbs and leafy vegetables — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate leafy herb category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fresh mint leaf exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Mint leaf contains only 4.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g with 7.2 g dietary fibre, yielding negligible available carbohydrate. A conservative GI of 45 (low category) is assigned by analogy with other low-carbohydrate leafy herbs in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 5 g serving = (45 × 0.44 × 5) / 100 = 0.1 g, which is nutritionally negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (28 mg/100 g) and high potassium (380 mg/100 g) providing a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Fresh mint leaf is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its very low sodium content (28 mg/100 g) and high potassium (380 mg/100 g) support blood pressure regulation, and it may be used freely as a flavouring to reduce reliance on salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low available carbohydrate (4.4 g/100 g) offset by high dietary fibre (7.2 g/100 g), resulting in negligible glycaemic impact at typical culinary serving sizes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fresh mint leaf poses no clinically meaningful glycaemic risk for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; at a typical 5 g serving the glycaemic load is approximately 0.1 g, and its high fibre content supports postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.2 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and high dietary fibre (7.2 g/100 g) with polyphenol content (rosmarinic acid, luteolin) supporting endothelial and lipid health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fresh mint leaf is cardioprotective in the context of a balanced diet; its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium, and polyphenol content (rosmarinic acid, luteolin) collectively support healthy lipid profiles and vascular function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in anti-inflammatory polyphenols (rosmarinic acid, luteolin) and high dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome health relevant to immune regulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mint leaf is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its polyphenol content (rosmarinic acid, luteolin) has documented anti-inflammatory properties, and its high fibre (7.2 g/100 g) supports gut microbiome diversity relevant to immune modulation in RA.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mint leaf (Mentha spp.) refers to the fresh leaves of culinary mint plants, most commonly spearmint (Mentha spicata) and peppermint (Mentha piperita). The leaves are bright green, tender, and aromatic, with a characteristic cool, sweet, and slightly menthol-like flavour derived from volatile oils including menthol, menthone, and limonene. Mint is used raw or lightly cooked across a wide range of cuisines — as a garnish in beverages (mojitos, teas, lassi), in salads, chutneys, raitas, tabbouleh, and as a flavouring in Southeast Asian dishes including Vietnamese pho and Singaporean rojak. Nutritionally, mint leaf is low in calories (54 kcal/100 g) and carbohydrates (4.4 g/100 g) but notably high in dietary fibre (7.2 g/100 g), calcium (210 mg/100 g), iron (3.6 mg/100 g), and potassium (380 mg/100 g). Its polyphenol content — particularly rosmarinic acid and luteolin — confers anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Clinically, mint leaf is suitable for individuals managing hypertension (very low sodium, high potassium), Type 2 Diabetes (negligible glycaemic load at culinary serving sizes), and cardiovascular disease (negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol). It is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. In Singapore, it is commonly known as pudina (Malay/Hindi) and 薄荷叶 (Chinese), and is widely available fresh in wet markets and supermarkets.
common_user_queries: Is mint leaf good for diabetes?
How many calories in fresh mint?
Can I eat mint leaves if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of mint?
What nutrients are in fresh mint leaves?
Is daun pudina the same as mint leaf?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173474 (Spearmint, fresh). Saturated fat and micronutrient values supplemented from USDA FDC with documented adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for low-carbohydrate leafy herbs, clearly documented.
notes: Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB and USDA FDC; mint leaf is a plant food and contains no cholesterol (naturally zero). Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg/100 g — naturally zero as this is a plant food. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg/100 g — naturally zero for fresh leafy herbs not exposed to UV treatment. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g — naturally zero for a raw plant food. Saturated fat (0.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.58 g/100 g), and micronutrient values (vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173474 (Spearmint, fresh) as HPB does not report these fields separately; values are consistent with the HPB primary macros. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' from the input cultural tags has been removed as it is not applicable to a fresh herb leaf. The dietary tag 'keto' is retained as mint leaf is very low in net carbohydrates. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 45, low category) as no direct measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for fresh mint leaf.
